findings.suggestion: Do not unpickle untrusted data. Replace with json.loads (returns only JSON-safe types) or a schema-validated deserializer. If a binary/object format is truly required, use a signed+verified envelope with a hard-restricted Unpickler.find_class allowlist, and validate that the result is actually a dict before returning.
findings.summary: load_state (source.py:6) takes an untrusted `blob: str`, base64-decodes it (line 10), and passes the bytes straight to pickle.loads (line 11). pickle deserialization executes arbitrary code via __reduce__/__setstate__, so any caller-controlled blob is remote code execution on the server. The inline comment (lines 7-9) acknowledges this as a BUG and says to 'Use JSON or a restricted schema instead', but the code still performs the unsafe unpickle — stated intent contradicts the implementation.
findings.title: Arbitrary code execution via pickle.loads on untrusted input

findings.suggestion: After safe decoding, assert/validate the result is a dict (e.g. `if not isinstance(result, dict): raise ValueError(...)`) before returning, or widen the annotation to match reality.
findings.summary: The signature load_state(blob: str) -> dict (source.py:6) promises a dict, but pickle.loads (line 11) returns whatever type the pickle stream encodes — any object, not necessarily a dict. The invariant is asserted but never checked, so downstream code trusting the `-> dict` contract can build on a false premise (independent of the RCE).
findings.title: Declared '-> dict' return invariant is not enforced

summary:
Risk-tiered review (lite) completed.

New file introduces load_state, which performs insecure deserialization of untrusted base64+pickle data — a critical, author-acknowledged RCE (source.py:11). The inline comment states the safe approach (JSON/restricted schema) but the code does the opposite, so stated intent contradicts implementation. Secondary assumption-integrity issue: the '-> dict' return type is an unenforced invariant since pickle.loads can yield any type. No fresh runtime evidence was attached, but the RCE is statically provable from the untrusted-input→pickle.loads data flow.

New file source.py adds load_state, which base64-decodes and pickle.loads a caller-supplied string. This is a critical insecure-deserialization / RCE sink. Currently 0 callers, so no user-facing flow is broken yet, but it is a latent critical defect that is exploitable the instant user input is routed to it. Reported as an obvious critical cross-scope defect per the specialist exception; no product-safety-scoped regressions found (no touched UI screens, states, or existing workflows).

Single new file source.py introduces load_state, an insecure deserialization sink: pickle.loads over base64-decoded caller input (source.py:10-11) — a critical RCE. The agent's own comment (source.py:7-9) flags it as a BUG and names the fix (JSON/restricted schema) yet the vulnerable code was shipped, a fake-green/incomplete-handoff pattern. load_state currently has 0 callers (newly added), which limits present blast radius, but the defect is intrinsic and must be fixed before any caller is wired up. No tests or fresh verification evidence exist; the rerun_relevant_verification gate remains blocked until a test proves a malicious payload cannot execute.
